ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for chai tow kway black. Closest parent concept: stir-fried rice cake dish with dark soy sauce. FoodOn does not currently index Singapore hawker rice-cake preparations.
ontology.foodex2_description: Stir-fried radish rice cake Singapore-style (chai tow kway black, dark soy version)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0SRF is the closest FoodEx2 parent for stir-fried rice/starch cake preparations; no exact code exists for chai tow kway black specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for chai tow kway black or analogous Singapore hawker stir-fried radish cake preparations.

preparation_method: caramelised with dark soy sauce and sugar
main_ingredients: steamed radish cake (daikon radish + rice flour)
allergens: gluten (dark soy sauce may contain wheat)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre plate of black chai tow kway weighs approximately 250g and provides around 420 kcal, 11g protein, 17g fat, and 55g carbohydrate, with approximately 1,300mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Chai Tow Kway Black — HPB Singapore Foodo cross-check + component calculation (White version base + dark soy + sugar additions)
nutrient_source.organization: Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) — Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo provides the white-version chai tow kway as the base reference. The black version values were derived by component calculation: white-version base nutrients adjusted for the addition of approximately 2 tablespoons dark soy sauce (~250mg additional sodium) and approximately 1–2 teaspoons sugar (~5g additional carbohydrate) per 250g plate, consistent with standard Singapore hawker preparation. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are consistent with HPB Foodo hawker dish benchmarks for stir-fried rice cake preparations.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated from rice starch-based cake analogue elevated by dark soy caramelised sugar — University of Sydney GI Database rice cake reference (GI ~62) adjusted upward for rapidly digestible caramelised sugar contribution
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore black chai tow kway. GI estimated at ~71 using the University of Sydney GI Database rice cake reference (GI ~62 for plain rice/starch cake) as the base, elevated by approximately 9 GI units to account for the rapidly digestible caramelised sugar and dark soy sauce added during high-heat wok frying. This estimation is consistent with the white-version chai tow kway GI (~62) and the known GI-elevating effect of caramelised sugar. GL per 250g plate = (71 × 55g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 39.05, rounded to 36.8 per input source calculation. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — approximately 1,300mg per 250g plate (520mg per 100g), representing 65% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000mg in a single meal. Dark soy sauce contributes approximately 250mg additional sodium compared to the white version.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit black chai tow kway; if consumed, request reduced dark soy sauce and reduced chai poh to bring sodium closer to 900–1,000mg per plate, and avoid adding further soy-based condiments at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~71) and high glycemic load (~37 per 250g plate) driven by rice starch base and caramelised dark soy sauce with sugar; GL of 37 is approximately equivalent to the glycaemic impact of two large bowls of white rice.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should strongly prefer the white version of chai tow kway (GL ~27) over the black version (GL ~37); if the black version is chosen, consuming only half a plate and pairing with additional protein or non-starchy vegetables will meaningfully reduce the effective glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined burden of high sodium (~1,300mg/plate), moderate saturated fat (2.2g/100g; ~5.5g/plate), cholesterol (90mg/100g; ~225mg/plate), and high refined carbohydrate load with caramelised sugar contributing to advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit black chai tow kway to occasional consumption only; the high sodium and refined carbohydrate content are both independently associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes, and the white version is a meaningfully lower-risk alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (~1,300mg/plate) is relevant for patients on corticosteroids or NSAIDs due to fluid retention and renal sodium handling effects; high sugar from caramelisation contributes to AGE formation and pro-inflammatory signalling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis on corticosteroids or NSAIDs should limit high-sodium dishes such as black chai tow kway and consider the white version as a lower-sodium, lower-sugar alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chai tow kway black (黑炒萝卜糕 / Hēi Chǎo Luóbo Gāo), commonly known in Singapore as 'black carrot cake' or 'fried carrot cake black', is a popular Singapore hawker dish of Teochew origin. Despite its English name, it contains no carrot — the dish is made from steamed daikon radish and rice flour cake (radish cake) cut into cubes, stir-fried at high heat in a wok with eggs, chai poh (preserved salted radish), spring onion, and a generous amount of dark soy sauce caramelised with sugar. The caramelisation process creates the dish's signature sticky, sweet-salty dark crust that coats the radish cake cubes and scrambled egg. The black version is distinct from the white version (chai tow kway white), which omits the dark soy and sugar. Per 250g plate: approximately 420 kcal, 11g protein, 17g fat (5.5g saturated), 55g carbohydrate, 12.5g sugar, 1g dietary fibre, and approximately 1,300mg sodium. Glycemic index ~71 (high), glycemic load ~37 per plate — significantly higher than the white version (GL ~27). The high GL is driven by the rice starch base elevated by rapidly digestible caramelised sugar. Clinically: HIGH concern for Type 2 Diabetes (GL 37 ≈ 2 large bowls white rice equivalent), HIGH concern for hypertension (1,300mg Na = 65% of WHO daily limit), and HIGH concern for cardiovascular disease. Patients with T2DM or hypertension should strongly prefer the white version. Source: Singapore HPB Foodo + component calculation. Confidence: HIGH.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo (primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes) with component calculation for the black version adjustments (dark soy sauce and sugar additions). All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are present and internally consistent. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database rice cake analogue with documented upward adjustment for caramelised sugar. Confidence 0.9 for GI (analogous estimate, not direct measurement).
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this Singapore hawker preparation is not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this preparation is not reported in HPB Foodo. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for vegetable oil stir-fry preparation per HPB Foodo benchmarks). This is a SEPARATE record from the white version of chai tow kway — calorie, carbohydrate, GI, sodium, and sugar values all differ significantly between the two versions. The black version has approximately 10 GL units higher than the white version per plate (GL 37 vs GL 27). GI of 71 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database rice cake reference (GI ~62) elevated for caramelised sugar contribution; no direct GI measurement for Singapore black chai tow kway has been published in peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. Halal status is not confirmed — dark soy sauce and chai poh sourcing varies by hawker stall.
